items.title: MFA is not enabled for Root Account
items.description: Ensure Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) is enabled for the AWS root account.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether the root user has an MFA device attached.

```
aws iam get-account-summary --query 'SummaryMap.AccountMFAEnabled'
```

A value of `0` means root MFA is OFF (finding). Confirm from the credential report:

```
aws iam generate-credential-report
aws iam get-credential-report --query 'Content' --output text | base64 -d | awk -F, 'NR==1 || $1=="<root_account>"'
```

Automated: Prowler check `iam_root_mfa_enabled` (`prowler aws -c iam_root_mfa_enabled`) or ScoutSuite IAM findings.
items.how_to_fix:
Sign in as root, open `IAM > My security credentials > Multi-factor authentication (MFA)`, and register a hardware/FIDO2 or virtual MFA device. Root MFA cannot be enabled via API. Verify:

```
aws iam get-account-summary --query 'SummaryMap.AccountMFAEnabled'
```

Expect `1`. Store root credentials offline, and add a CloudWatch alarm / SCP that alerts on root usage. Re-scan with `prowler aws -c iam_root_mfa_enabled`.

items.title: MFA is not enabled for IAM Users
items.description: Ensure Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) is enabled for all AWS IAM users with AWS Console access.
items.how_to_find:
List every console user and whether an MFA device is registered:

```
for u in $(aws iam list-users --query 'Users[].UserName' --output text); do echo "$u MFA=$(aws iam list-mfa-devices --user-name "$u" --query 'length(MFADevices)')"; done
```

Any user with `MFA=0` and console access is a finding. At scale, use the credential report column `mfa_active`:

```
aws iam get-credential-report --query 'Content' --output text | base64 -d | awk -F, 'NR>1{print $1, $4, $8}'
```

Automated: Prowler `iam_user_mfa_enabled_console_access`.
items.how_to_fix:
Require MFA for all human users and deny actions when MFA is absent:

```
{"Effect":"Deny","NotAction":["iam:CreateVirtualMFADevice","iam:EnableMFADevice","iam:ListMFADevices","sts:GetSessionToken"],"Resource":"*","Condition":{"BoolIfExists":{"aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent":"false"}}}
```

Prefer migrating humans to IAM Identity Center (SSO) with enforced MFA. Verify with the credential report and `prowler aws -c iam_user_mfa_enabled_console_access`.

items.title: Multiple Access Keys exists for IAM Users
items.description: Detects when a canary token access key has been used.
items.how_to_find:
Find users with two active access keys (a rotation/abuse risk):

```
for u in $(aws iam list-users --query 'Users[].UserName' --output text); do n=$(aws iam list-access-keys --user-name "$u" --query 'length(AccessKeyMetadata[?Status==`Active`])'); [ "$n" -gt 1 ] && echo "$u has $n active keys"; done
```

Automated: Prowler `iam_no_active_access_keys_multiple` / review the credential report `access_key_1_active` and `access_key_2_active` columns.
items.how_to_fix:
Keep at most one active key per user; delete the unused one after confirming it is not in use (check `AccessKeyLastUsed`):

```
aws iam get-access-key-last-used --access-key-id <AKIA...>
aws iam update-access-key --user-name <user> --access-key-id <AKIA...> --status Inactive
aws iam delete-access-key --user-name <user> --access-key-id <AKIA...>
```

Better: replace long-lived keys with IAM roles / IAM Identity Center short-lived credentials. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Cross-Account Access Lacks External ID and MFA
items.description: Ensure cross-account IAM roles use either MFA or external IDs to secure the access to AWS resources.
items.how_to_find:
Inspect every role whose trust policy trusts another account and check for an `sts:ExternalId` or `aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent` condition:

```
for r in $(aws iam list-roles --query 'Roles[].RoleName' --output text); do aws iam get-role --role-name "$r" --query 'Role.AssumeRolePolicyDocument' --output json | grep -q 'AWS' && echo "=== $r ===" && aws iam get-role --role-name "$r" --query 'Role.AssumeRolePolicyDocument'; done
```

A cross-account `Principal.AWS` trust with no `Condition` is the finding. Automated: Prowler `iam_role_cross_account_readonlyaccess_policy` / `iam_role_cross_service_confused_deputy_prevention`.
items.how_to_fix:
Add an `ExternalId` (for third parties) and/or require MFA in the role trust policy:

```
{"Effect":"Allow","Principal":{"AWS":"arn:aws:iam::<partner>:root"},"Action":"sts:AssumeRole","Condition":{"StringEquals":{"sts:ExternalId":"<unguessable-id>"},"Bool":{"aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent":"true"}}}
```

Update via `aws iam update-assume-role-policy --role-name <r> --policy-document file://trust.json`. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Lack of Access Key Rotation
items.description: Ensure AWS IAM access keys are rotated on a periodic basis as a security best practice (90 Days).
items.how_to_find:
Flag access keys older than 90 days using the credential report or per key:

```
aws iam list-access-keys --user-name <user> --query 'AccessKeyMetadata[].[AccessKeyId,CreateDate]' --output table
```

Credential report at scale:

```
aws iam get-credential-report --query 'Content' --output text | base64 -d | awk -F, 'NR>1{print $1,$10,$12}'
```

Columns `access_key_1_last_rotated` older than 90 days are findings. Automated: Prowler `iam_rotate_access_key_90_days`.
items.how_to_fix:
Rotate on a <=90-day schedule: create a new key, deploy it, then disable/delete the old one:

```
aws iam create-access-key --user-name <user>
aws iam update-access-key --user-name <user> --access-key-id <OLD> --status Inactive
aws iam delete-access-key --user-name <user> --access-key-id <OLD>
```

Prefer eliminating static keys via roles/Identity Center. Enforce with a scheduled Config rule `access-keys-rotated`. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Password Expiration is Disabled
items.description: Ensure AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) user passwords are reset before expiration (90 Days).
items.how_to_find:
Check the account password policy for `MaxPasswordAge`:

```
aws iam get-account-password-policy --query 'PasswordPolicy'
```

Missing `MaxPasswordAge` (or `>90`) is a finding; `NoSuchEntity` means no policy at all. Automated: Prowler `iam_password_policy_expires_passwords_within_90_days`.
items.how_to_fix:
Set a password policy that expires passwords within 90 days:

```
aws iam update-account-password-policy --max-password-age 90 --minimum-password-length 14 --require-symbols --require-numbers --require-uppercase-characters --require-lowercase-characters --password-reuse-prevention 24
```

Ideally move humans to federated SSO so console passwords disappear. Verify with `aws iam get-account-password-policy` and re-scan.

items.title: Weak Password Policy ( AWS Default password policy) is set for the AWS account
items.description: Ensure AWS account has an IAM strong password policy in use.
items.how_to_find:
Review the current password policy against CIS (length >= 14, complexity, reuse prevention):

```
aws iam get-account-password-policy --query 'PasswordPolicy'
```

Defaults / short lengths / no reuse prevention are findings. Automated: Prowler `iam_password_policy_*` checks.
items.how_to_fix:
Apply a strong policy:

```
aws iam update-account-password-policy --minimum-password-length 14 --require-symbols --require-numbers --require-uppercase-characters --require-lowercase-characters --password-reuse-prevention 24 --max-password-age 90
```

Manage via IaC (Terraform `aws_iam_account_password_policy`) so it cannot drift. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Weak IAM Server Certificate in use
items.description: Ensure that all your SSL/TLS certificates are using either 2048 or 4096 bit RSA keys instead of 1024-bit keys.
items.how_to_find:
List uploaded IAM server certificates and inspect key size / signature algorithm:

```
aws iam list-server-certificates --query 'ServerCertificateMetadataList[].[ServerCertificateName,Expiration]' --output table
aws iam get-server-certificate --server-certificate-name <name> --query 'ServerCertificate.CertificateBody' --output text | openssl x509 -noout -text | grep -E 'Public-Key|Signature Algorithm'
```

RSA < 2048-bit or SHA-1 signatures are findings.
items.how_to_fix:
Replace weak certs with ACM-managed certificates (auto-renewed, RSA-2048/ECDSA):

```
aws acm request-certificate --domain-name <fqdn> --validation-method DNS
```

Attach the ACM cert to the ELB/CloudFront and delete the old IAM cert with `aws iam delete-server-certificate --server-certificate-name <name>`. Prefer ACM over uploaded IAM certs entirely.

items.title: IAM Role Policy are Too Permissive
items.description: Ensure AWS IAM policies attached to IAM roles are not too permissive.
items.how_to_find:
Hunt for wildcard actions/resources in customer-managed policies:

```
for p in $(aws iam list-policies --scope Local --query 'Policies[].Arn' --output text); do v=$(aws iam get-policy --policy-arn "$p" --query 'Policy.DefaultVersionId' --output text); aws iam get-policy-version --policy-arn "$p" --version-id "$v" --query 'PolicyVersion.Document' --output json | grep -q '"Action": "\*"' && echo "WILDCARD in $p"; done
```

Use IAM Access Analyzer policy validation and last-accessed data. Automated: Prowler `iam_policy_allows_privilege_escalation`, `iam_inline_policy_allows_privilege_escalation`.
items.how_to_fix:
Right-size policies using Access Analyzer generated policies from CloudTrail activity:

```
aws accessanalyzer start-policy-generation --policy-generation-details '{"principalArn":"arn:aws:iam::<acct>:role/<role>"}' --cloud-trail-details ...
```

Remove `"Action":"*"` / `"Resource":"*"`, scope to specific ARNs, and add conditions. Validate before apply:

```
aws accessanalyzer validate-policy --policy-document file://policy.json --policy-type IDENTITY_POLICY
```

Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: IAM Access Analyzer is not Enabled
items.description: Ensure that IAM Access Analyzer feature is enabled to maintain access security to your AWS resources.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether an account/organization analyzer exists in each region:

```
aws accessanalyzer list-analyzers --query 'analyzers[].[name,type,status]' --output table
```

Empty output = finding. Automated: Prowler `accessanalyzer_enabled`.
items.how_to_fix:
Create an account (or org-wide) analyzer:

```
aws accessanalyzer create-analyzer --analyzer-name account-analyzer --type ACCOUNT
```

For multi-account, create a `ORGANIZATION` analyzer in the delegated admin. Triage external-access findings and archive expected ones. Deploy per region via IaC. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Pre-Heartbleed Server Certificates
items.description: Ensure that your server certificates are not vulnerable to Heartbleed security bug.
items.how_to_find:
Identify IAM server certificates uploaded before the Heartbleed disclosure (2014-04) that may use vulnerable key material:

```
aws iam list-server-certificates --query 'ServerCertificateMetadataList[].[ServerCertificateName,UploadDate,Expiration]' --output table
```

Certs uploaded pre-2014-04-07 should be treated as suspect.
items.how_to_fix:
Reissue and replace affected certificates with fresh ACM-managed certs, then delete the old ones:

```
aws acm request-certificate --domain-name <fqdn> --validation-method DNS
aws iam delete-server-certificate --server-certificate-name <old>
```

Rotate any private keys/secrets that could have been exposed.

items.title: Root Account Access Keys Present
items.description: Ensure that your AWS account (root) is not using access keys as a security best practice.
items.how_to_find:
Check the account summary for root access keys (there must be zero):

```
aws iam get-account-summary --query 'SummaryMap.AccountAccessKeysPresent'
```

`1` is a critical finding. Confirm via credential report columns `access_key_1_active`/`access_key_2_active` for the `<root_account>` row. Automated: Prowler `iam_no_root_access_key`.
items.how_to_fix:
Delete root access keys entirely (root should never have programmatic keys). As root, go to `My security credentials > Access keys` and delete them, then verify:

```
aws iam get-account-summary --query 'SummaryMap.AccountAccessKeysPresent'
```

Expect `0`. Use IAM roles / Identity Center for automation instead. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Lack of SSH Public Keys Rotation
items.description: Ensure IAM SSH public keys are rotated on a periodic basis to adhere to AWS security best practices.
items.how_to_find:
List CodeCommit SSH public keys per user and their upload dates to flag stale keys (>90 days):

```
aws iam list-ssh-public-keys --user-name <user> --query 'SSHPublicKeys[].[SSHPublicKeyId,Status,UploadDate]' --output table
```

Keys older than your rotation window are findings.
items.how_to_fix:
Rotate SSH keys: upload a new one, then deactivate/delete the old:

```
aws iam upload-ssh-public-key --user-name <user> --ssh-public-key-body file://new_key.pub
aws iam update-ssh-public-key --user-name <user> --ssh-public-key-id <OLD> --status Inactive
aws iam delete-ssh-public-key --user-name <user> --ssh-public-key-id <OLD>
```

Prefer git-remote-codecommit with IAM creds or CodeCatalyst to avoid static SSH keys.

items.title: SSL/TLS Certificate is about to Expire
items.description: Ensure SSL/TLS certificates are renewed before their expiration.
items.how_to_find:
List ACM certificates and their expiry, flagging those expiring within 30 days:

```
aws acm list-certificates --query 'CertificateSummaryList[].CertificateArn' --output text | tr '\t' '\n' | while read a; do aws acm describe-certificate --certificate-arn "$a" --query 'Certificate.[DomainName,NotAfter,RenewalEligibility]' --output text; done
```

Automated: Prowler `acm_certificates_expiration_check`.
items.how_to_fix:
Use ACM DNS-validated certificates so renewal is automatic. For imported certs, reissue and re-import before expiry:

```
aws acm import-certificate --certificate file://cert.pem --private-key file://key.pem --certificate-chain file://chain.pem
```

Add an EventBridge rule on ACM `Certificate Approaching Expiration` to alert. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Security Challenge Question not Enabled
items.description: Ensure security challenge questions are enabled and configured to improve the security of your AWS account.
items.how_to_find: Challenge/security questions are configured only in the console (no API). Sign in as root and check `Account settings > Security challenge questions`. Missing questions is a low-severity finding.
items.how_to_fix: As root, set three security challenge questions under `Account settings`, using answers stored in your password manager (not guessable/PII). Document the recovery runbook.

items.title: Security Contact Information is not Registered
items.description: Ensure alternate contacts are set to improve the security of your AWS account.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether an alternate security contact is registered:

```
aws account get-alternate-contact --alternate-contact-type SECURITY
```

`ResourceNotFound` = finding. Automated: Prowler `account_security_contact_information_is_registered`.
items.how_to_fix:
Register a monitored security contact (distribution list, not a person):

```
aws account put-alternate-contact --alternate-contact-type SECURITY --email-address security@corp.example --name "Security Team" --phone-number "+1..." --title "Security"
```

Re-verify with `aws account get-alternate-contact --alternate-contact-type SECURITY`.

items.title: AWS Multi-Account are managed centrally via Identity Federation or AWS Organization
items.description: Set up, organize and manage your AWS accounts for optimal security and manageability.
items.how_to_find:
Confirm the account belongs to an Organization and uses centralized identity:

```
aws organizations describe-organization --query 'Organization.[Id,FeatureSet]'
aws sso-admin list-instances
```

Standalone accounts with local IAM users instead of Identity Center are findings.
items.how_to_fix: Bring accounts under AWS Organizations (all-features), enable IAM Identity Center for human access with an external IdP, and apply SCP guardrails. Replace local IAM users with permission sets. Verify with `aws organizations list-accounts` and `aws sso-admin list-instances`.

items.title: Root Account Recently used
items.description: Ensure root account credentials have not been used recently to access your AWS account.
items.how_to_find:
Check when root last signed in / used credentials via the credential report:

```
aws iam get-credential-report --query 'Content' --output text | base64 -d | awk -F, 'NR==1 || $1=="<root_account>"' | cut -d, -f1,5,11
```

Recent `password_last_used` / `access_key_last_used` for root is a critical finding. Also query CloudTrail:

```
aws cloudtrail lookup-events --lookup-attributes AttributeKey=Username,AttributeValue=root --max-results 10
```

Automated: Prowler `iam_no_root_access_key` + CloudTrail monitoring.
items.how_to_fix:
Root should be used only for the few tasks that require it. Investigate the CloudTrail events, rotate root password + MFA if usage was unexpected, and add a CloudWatch metric filter + alarm on `userIdentity.type = Root`:

```
{ $.userIdentity.type = "Root" && $.userIdentity.invokedBy NOT EXISTS && $.eventType != "AwsServiceEvent" }
```

Enforce via SCP that denies most actions for the root principal in member accounts.

sections.title: Elastic Compute Cloud (EC2) , Elastic Blob Storage (EBS), Elastic Load Balancer V2 (ELBv2)

items.title: Overbroad Ingress Rules for Security Groups
items.description: Ensure no EC2 security group allows unrestricted inbound access to any uncommon ports.
items.how_to_find:
Find security groups allowing `0.0.0.0/0` (or `::/0`) to sensitive ports (22, 3389, 3306, 5432, etc.):

```
aws ec2 describe-security-groups --query "SecurityGroups[?IpPermissions[?(FromPort==`22` || FromPort==`3389`) && contains(IpRanges[].CidrIp, '0.0.0.0/0')]].[GroupId,GroupName]" --output table
```

Automated: Prowler `ec2_securitygroup_allow_ingress_from_internet_to_any_port`, `ec2_securitygroup_allow_ingress_from_internet_to_port_22`.
items.how_to_fix:
Restrict ingress to known CIDRs / prefix lists, and use SSM Session Manager instead of open SSH/RDP. Remove the offending rule:

```
aws ec2 revoke-security-group-ingress --group-id <sg> --protocol tcp --port 22 --cidr 0.0.0.0/0
aws ec2 authorize-security-group-ingress --group-id <sg> --protocol tcp --port 22 --cidr <office-cidr>
```

Manage SGs in Terraform and add a Config rule `restricted-ssh`. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: EC2 Instance Termination Protection is not Enabled
items.description: Ensure Termination Protection feature is enabled for EC2 instances that are not part of ASGs.
items.how_to_find:
List instances and their `disableApiTermination` attribute:

```
for i in $(aws ec2 describe-instances --query 'Reservations[].Instances[].InstanceId' --output text); do echo "$i $(aws ec2 describe-instance-attribute --instance-id "$i" --attribute disableApiTermination --query 'DisableApiTermination.Value')"; done
```

`False` on production instances is a finding.
items.how_to_fix:
Enable termination protection on production/stateful instances:

```
aws ec2 modify-instance-attribute --instance-id <id> --disable-api-termination
```

Set `disable_api_termination = true` in Terraform for prod. Combine with `disableApiStop` for critical hosts.

items.title: AMIs are Publicaly Shared
items.description: Ensure your Amazon Machine Images (AMIs) are not accessible to all AWS accounts.
items.how_to_find:
List AMIs you own that are shared publicly:

```
aws ec2 describe-images --owners self --query "Images[?Public==`true`].[ImageId,Name]" --output table
```

Any public AMI may leak baked-in secrets/data. Automated: Prowler `ec2_ami_public`.
items.how_to_fix:
Make the AMI private and rotate anything it may have exposed:

```
aws ec2 modify-image-attribute --image-id <ami> --launch-permission "Remove=[{Group=all}]"
```

Share to specific account IDs instead of `all`, and scan AMIs for secrets before publishing. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Golden/Approved AMIs not in Use
items.description: Ensure all AWS EC2 instances are launched from approved AMIs.
items.how_to_find:
Compare running instances' AMIs against your approved/golden AMI list:

```
aws ec2 describe-instances --query 'Reservations[].Instances[].[InstanceId,ImageId]' --output table
```

Instances not built from a hardened, patched golden AMI are findings.
items.how_to_fix: Build hardened golden AMIs with EC2 Image Builder (CIS-based), tag them `approved=true`, and enforce their use via launch templates and an SCP/Config rule `approved-amis-by-id`. Rebuild/patch on a schedule and re-bake.

items.title: Amazon EBS Snapshots are Publicly Accessible
items.description: Ensure that your Amazon EBS volume snapshots are not accessible to all AWS accounts.
items.how_to_find:
List snapshots you own that are shared with `all`:

```
aws ec2 describe-snapshots --owner-ids self --query 'Snapshots[].SnapshotId' --output text | tr '\t' '\n' | while read s; do aws ec2 describe-snapshot-attribute --snapshot-id "$s" --attribute createVolumePermission --query "CreateVolumePermissions[?Group=='all']" --output text | grep -q all && echo "PUBLIC $s"; done
```

Critical: public snapshots expose full disk contents. Automated: Prowler `ec2_ebs_public_snapshot`.
items.how_to_fix:
Remove public share and enable account-level public-access block for snapshots:

```
aws ec2 modify-snapshot-attribute --snapshot-id <snap> --attribute createVolumePermission --operation-type remove --group-names all
aws ec2 enable-snapshot-block-public-access --state block-all-sharing
```

Rotate any secrets that resided on the exposed volume. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Amazon EBS Volumes Encryption is not enabled
items.description: Ensure that existing Elastic Block Store (EBS) attached volumes are encrypted to meet security and compliance requirements.
items.how_to_find:
List unencrypted volumes:

```
aws ec2 describe-volumes --query "Volumes[?Encrypted==`false`].[VolumeId,State]" --output table
```

Automated: Prowler `ec2_ebs_volume_encryption`.
items.how_to_fix:
Enable EBS encryption by default for the region, then re-create unencrypted volumes from encrypted snapshots:

```
aws ec2 enable-ebs-encryption-by-default
aws ec2 create-snapshot --volume-id <vol>
aws ec2 copy-snapshot --source-snapshot-id <snap> --encrypted --kms-key-id <cmk> --source-region <region>
```

Create a new volume from the encrypted snapshot and swap it in. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Amazon EBS Snapshots Encryption is not enabled
items.description: Ensure Amazon EBS snapshots are encrypted to meet security and compliance requirements.
items.how_to_find:
List unencrypted snapshots you own:

```
aws ec2 describe-snapshots --owner-ids self --query "Snapshots[?Encrypted==`false`].[SnapshotId,VolumeId]" --output table
```

Automated: Prowler `ec2_ebs_snapshots_encrypted`.
items.how_to_fix:
Copy to an encrypted snapshot with a CMK and delete the unencrypted original:

```
aws ec2 copy-snapshot --source-region <region> --source-snapshot-id <snap> --encrypted --kms-key-id <cmk>
aws ec2 delete-snapshot --snapshot-id <snap>
```

Turn on `enable-ebs-encryption-by-default` so future snapshots inherit encryption. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: KMS Customer Master Keys is not used for EBS Volume Encryption
items.description: Ensure EBS volumes are encrypted with KMS CMKs in order to have full control over data encryption and decryption.
items.how_to_find:
Check which volumes use the AWS-managed `aws/ebs` key vs a customer-managed CMK:

```
aws ec2 describe-volumes --query 'Volumes[?Encrypted==`true`].[VolumeId,KmsKeyId]' --output table
```

KMS ARNs ending in `alias/aws/ebs` (or the default key) are findings where a CMK is required.
items.how_to_fix:
Set a customer-managed CMK as the default EBS encryption key and re-encrypt via snapshot copy:

```
aws ec2 modify-ebs-default-kms-key-id --kms-key-id <cmk-arn>
aws ec2 copy-snapshot --source-snapshot-id <snap> --encrypted --kms-key-id <cmk-arn> --source-region <region>
```

Grant key usage to the relevant roles in the CMK policy. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Weak Cryptographic Controls for ELB
items.description: Ensure AWS Application Load Balancers (ALBs) are using the latest predefined security policy.
items.how_to_find:
Check the SSL negotiation/security policy on HTTPS/TLS listeners:

```
aws elbv2 describe-listeners --load-balancer-arn <alb-arn> --query 'Listeners[?Protocol==`HTTPS`].[ListenerArn,SslPolicy]' --output table
```

Policies allowing TLS 1.0/1.1 or weak ciphers are findings. Automated: Prowler `elbv2_ssl_listeners`, `elb_ssl_listeners`.
items.how_to_fix:
Apply a modern TLS 1.2+/1.3 policy to the listener:

```
aws elbv2 modify-listener --listener-arn <listener> --ssl-policy ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-2021-06
```

Redirect HTTP:80 to HTTPS:443 and enable HSTS at the app. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Load Balancer is not Integrated with AWS WAF
items.description: Ensure that WAF ACL is integrated with Elastic Load Balancer.
items.how_to_find:
List ALBs and check for an associated WAFv2 web ACL:

```
for lb in $(aws elbv2 describe-load-balancers --query "LoadBalancers[?Type=='application'].LoadBalancerArn" --output text); do echo "$lb -> $(aws wafv2 get-web-acl-for-resource --resource-arn "$lb" --query 'WebACL.Name' --output text 2>/dev/null)"; done
```

`None`/empty = finding. Automated: Prowler `elbv2_waf_acl_attached`.
items.how_to_fix:
Create/associate a WAFv2 web ACL with AWS managed rule groups and attach it:

```
aws wafv2 associate-web-acl --web-acl-arn <acl-arn> --resource-arn <alb-arn>
```

Include `AWSManagedRulesCommonRuleSet` + rate-based rules. Manage in IaC and re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: ELB uses In-secure protocols
items.description: Ensure that your Application Load Balancer (ALB) listeners are using a secure protocol such as HTTPS.
items.how_to_find:
Detect listeners still serving plain HTTP or TCP without TLS:

```
aws elbv2 describe-listeners --load-balancer-arn <alb-arn> --query 'Listeners[].[Protocol,Port]' --output table
```

An `HTTP` listener that does not redirect to HTTPS is a finding.
items.how_to_fix:
Convert HTTP listeners to a redirect and terminate TLS on 443:

```
aws elbv2 modify-listener --listener-arn <http-listener> --default-actions '[{"Type":"redirect","RedirectConfig":{"Protocol":"HTTPS","Port":"443","StatusCode":"HTTP_301"}}]'
```

Use an ACM cert on the HTTPS listener with a TLS1.2+ policy. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: ELB Deletion Protection Disabled
items.description: Ensure Deletion Protection feature is enabled for your AWS load balancers to follow security best practices.
items.how_to_find:
Check the `deletion_protection.enabled` attribute:

```
aws elbv2 describe-load-balancer-attributes --load-balancer-arn <alb-arn> --query "Attributes[?Key=='deletion_protection.enabled']"
```

`false` on production LBs is a finding.
items.how_to_fix:
Enable deletion protection:

```
aws elbv2 modify-load-balancer-attributes --load-balancer-arn <alb-arn> --attributes Key=deletion_protection.enabled,Value=true
```

Set `enable_deletion_protection = true` in Terraform for prod LBs.

items.title: Access logging disabled for ELB
items.description: Ensure access logging is enabled for your AWS ALBs to follow security best practices.
items.how_to_find:
Check the access-logs attribute:

```
aws elbv2 describe-load-balancer-attributes --load-balancer-arn <alb-arn> --query "Attributes[?Key=='access_logs.s3.enabled']"
```

`false` = finding (no request-level audit trail). Automated: Prowler `elbv2_logging_enabled`.
items.how_to_fix:
Enable access logs to a hardened, encrypted S3 bucket with the correct bucket policy:

```
aws elbv2 modify-load-balancer-attributes --load-balancer-arn <alb-arn> --attributes Key=access_logs.s3.enabled,Value=true Key=access_logs.s3.bucket,Value=<log-bucket>
```

Set a lifecycle policy and ship to your SIEM. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: VPC Flow Logs Disabled
items.description: Ensure Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) Flow Logs feature is enabled in all applicable AWS regions.
items.how_to_find:
List VPCs without flow logs:

```
for v in $(aws ec2 describe-vpcs --query 'Vpcs[].VpcId' --output text); do n=$(aws ec2 describe-flow-logs --filter Name=resource-id,Values="$v" --query 'length(FlowLogs)'); echo "$v flowlogs=$n"; done
```

`flowlogs=0` = finding. Automated: Prowler `vpc_flow_logs_enabled`.
items.how_to_fix:
Enable flow logs (ALL traffic) to CloudWatch Logs or S3:

```
aws ec2 create-flow-logs --resource-type VPC --resource-ids <vpc> --traffic-type ALL --log-destination-type s3 --log-destination arn:aws:s3:::<log-bucket>
```

Enable org-wide via IaC/Config, retain per policy, and forward to your SIEM. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Overbroad S3 Access Control
items.description: Ensure that your AWS S3 buckets are not publicly exposed to the Internet.
items.how_to_find:
Enumerate buckets and check public-access block, ACLs, and policy:

```
for b in $(aws s3api list-buckets --query 'Buckets[].Name' --output text); do echo "=== $b ==="; aws s3api get-public-access-block --bucket "$b" --query 'PublicAccessBlockConfiguration' 2>/dev/null; aws s3api get-bucket-acl --bucket "$b" --query "Grants[?Grantee.URI!=null]"; done
```

Grants to `AllUsers`/`AuthenticatedUsers` or `"Principal":"*"` policies are findings. Automated: Prowler `s3_bucket_public_access`, `s3_account_level_public_access_blocks`.
items.how_to_fix:
Turn on account-level and bucket-level Block Public Access and remove public grants:

```
aws s3control put-public-access-block --account-id <acct> --public-access-block-configuration BlockPublicAcls=true,IgnorePublicAcls=true,BlockPublicPolicy=true,RestrictPublicBuckets=true
aws s3api put-public-access-block --bucket <b> --public-access-block-configuration BlockPublicAcls=true,IgnorePublicAcls=true,BlockPublicPolicy=true,RestrictPublicBuckets=true
```

Use bucket policies with explicit principals + `aws:PrincipalOrgID`. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Cross-Account Access for S3 Buckets
items.description: Ensure Amazon S3 buckets do not allow unknown cross account access via bucket policies.
items.how_to_find:
Review each bucket policy for external account principals:

```
aws s3api get-bucket-policy --bucket <b> --query Policy --output text | python3 -m json.tool
```

`Principal.AWS` ARNs from accounts outside your Organization are findings. Cross-check with IAM Access Analyzer external-access findings:

```
aws accessanalyzer list-findings --analyzer-arn <arn> --filter '{"resourceType":{"eq":["AWS::S3::Bucket"]}}'
```
items.how_to_fix:
Scope bucket policies to specific known accounts and add an `aws:PrincipalOrgID` condition so only your org can access:

```
{"Effect":"Allow","Principal":{"AWS":"arn:aws:iam::<acct>:root"},"Action":"s3:GetObject","Resource":"arn:aws:s3:::<b>/*","Condition":{"StringEquals":{"aws:PrincipalOrgID":"o-xxxx"}}}
```

Archive expected Access Analyzer findings and alert on new ones. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Server-Side Encryption is not Enabled for S3
items.description: Ensure AWS S3 buckets enforce Server-Side Encryption (SSE).
items.how_to_find:
Check default encryption on each bucket:

```
aws s3api get-bucket-encryption --bucket <b> --query 'ServerSideEncryptionConfiguration.Rules'
```

`ServerSideEncryptionConfigurationNotFoundError` = finding. Automated: Prowler `s3_bucket_default_encryption`.
items.how_to_fix:
Enable default encryption (SSE-KMS preferred) and enforce it via policy:

```
aws s3api put-bucket-encryption --bucket <b> --server-side-encryption-configuration '{"Rules":[{"ApplyServerSideEncryptionByDefault":{"SSEAlgorithm":"aws:kms","KMSMasterKeyID":"<cmk>"},"BucketKeyEnabled":true}]}'
```

Add a `Deny` policy for `s3:PutObject` when `s3:x-amz-server-side-encryption` is absent. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: KMS Customer Master Keys is not used for S3 Buckets Encryption
items.description: Ensure that Amazon S3 buckets are encrypted with customer-provided AWS KMS CMKs.
items.how_to_find:
Identify buckets encrypted with SSE-S3 (`AES256`) rather than a KMS CMK:

```
aws s3api get-bucket-encryption --bucket <b> --query 'ServerSideEncryptionConfiguration.Rules[].ApplyServerSideEncryptionByDefault'
```

`SSEAlgorithm=AES256` where a CMK is required is a finding. Automated: Prowler `s3_bucket_kms_encryption`.
items.how_to_fix:
Switch default encryption to a customer-managed CMK with bucket keys enabled:

```
aws s3api put-bucket-encryption --bucket <b> --server-side-encryption-configuration '{"Rules":[{"ApplyServerSideEncryptionByDefault":{"SSEAlgorithm":"aws:kms","KMSMasterKeyID":"<cmk-arn>"},"BucketKeyEnabled":true}]}'
```

Grant `kms:Decrypt`/`kms:GenerateDataKey` to consuming roles in the key policy. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Versioning and Multi-Factor Delete is not Enabled on S3 buckets
items.description: Ensure AWS S3 buckets have the MFA Delete feature enabled.
items.how_to_find:
Check versioning and MFA delete status:

```
aws s3api get-bucket-versioning --bucket <b>
```

`Status != Enabled` or `MFADelete != Enabled` is a finding for critical buckets. Automated: Prowler `s3_bucket_object_versioning`.
items.how_to_fix:
Enable versioning, then enable MFA delete (must be done by the bucket-owner root with an MFA device):

```
aws s3api put-bucket-versioning --bucket <b> --versioning-configuration Status=Enabled
aws s3api put-bucket-versioning --bucket <b> --versioning-configuration Status=Enabled,MFADelete=Enabled --mfa "arn:aws:iam::<acct>:mfa/root-account-mfa-device 123456"
```

Add lifecycle rules to expire noncurrent versions. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Access Logging Disabled for S3
items.description: Ensure AWS S3 buckets have server access logging enabled to track access requests.
items.how_to_find:
Check server access logging (or CloudTrail data events) per bucket:

```
aws s3api get-bucket-logging --bucket <b>
```

Empty result = finding. Automated: Prowler `s3_bucket_server_access_logging_enabled`.
items.how_to_fix:
Enable server access logging to a dedicated log bucket, and/or CloudTrail S3 data events for richer auditing:

```
aws s3api put-bucket-logging --bucket <b> --bucket-logging-status '{"LoggingEnabled":{"TargetBucket":"<log-bucket>","TargetPrefix":"<b>/"}}'
```

Ship logs to your SIEM with lifecycle retention. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Secure Transport is Not Enabled on S3
items.description: Ensure AWS S3 buckets enforce SSL to secure data in transit.
items.how_to_find:
Check the bucket policy denies non-TLS requests (`aws:SecureTransport=false`):

```
aws s3api get-bucket-policy --bucket <b> --query Policy --output text | python3 -m json.tool | grep -i securetransport
```

No such deny statement = finding. Automated: Prowler `s3_bucket_secure_transport_policy`.
items.how_to_fix:
Add a deny-in-clear policy statement:

```
{"Sid":"DenyInsecureTransport","Effect":"Deny","Principal":"*","Action":"s3:*","Resource":["arn:aws:s3:::<b>","arn:aws:s3:::<b>/*"],"Condition":{"Bool":{"aws:SecureTransport":"false"}}}
```

Apply with `aws s3api put-bucket-policy`. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: CloudTrail Log Encryption Disabled
items.description: Ensure your AWS CloudTrail logs are encrypted using AWS KMS–Managed Keys (SSE-KMS).
items.how_to_find:
List trails and check for a `KmsKeyId` (SSE-KMS on log files):

```
aws cloudtrail describe-trails --query 'trailList[].[Name,KmsKeyId]' --output table
```

Empty `KmsKeyId` means logs use SSE-S3 only. Automated: Prowler `cloudtrail_kms_encryption_enabled`.
items.how_to_fix:
Encrypt trail log files with a CMK:

```
aws cloudtrail update-trail --name <trail> --kms-key-id <cmk-arn>
```

Ensure the CMK policy allows `cloudtrail.amazonaws.com` to `GenerateDataKey`. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: KMS Customer Master Keys is not used for CloudTrail Encryption
items.description: Ensure that KMS master keys are used for CloudTrail Encryption.
items.how_to_find:
Confirm each trail uses a customer-managed CMK (not just SSE-S3):

```
aws cloudtrail get-trail --name <trail> --query 'Trail.KmsKeyId'
```

`null` = finding. Automated: Prowler `cloudtrail_kms_encryption_enabled`.
items.how_to_fix:
Attach a customer-managed CMK to the trail and grant CloudTrail usage in the key policy:

```
aws cloudtrail update-trail --name <trail> --kms-key-id arn:aws:kms:<region>:<acct>:key/<id>
```

Restrict `kms:Decrypt` on the key to the security/audit roles. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: CloudTrail Log File Validation is Disabled
items.description: Ensure your AWS CloudTrail trails have log file integrity validation enabled.
items.how_to_find:
Check the `LogFileValidationEnabled` flag per trail:

```
aws cloudtrail describe-trails --query 'trailList[].[Name,LogFileValidationEnabled]' --output table
```

`False` = finding (no tamper detection). Automated: Prowler `cloudtrail_log_file_validation_enabled`.
items.how_to_fix:
Enable log file integrity validation and periodically verify digests:

```
aws cloudtrail update-trail --name <trail> --enable-log-file-validation
aws cloudtrail validate-logs --trail-arn <arn> --start-time <iso8601>
```

Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: CloudTrail is not integrated with CloudWatch
items.description: Ensure CloudTrail event monitoring with CloudWatch is enabled.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether the trail delivers to a CloudWatch Logs group:

```
aws cloudtrail describe-trails --query 'trailList[].[Name,CloudWatchLogsLogGroupArn]' --output table
```

`null` = finding (no real-time metric filters/alarms). Automated: Prowler `cloudtrail_cloudwatch_logging_enabled`.
items.how_to_fix:
Wire the trail to CloudWatch Logs and add metric filters/alarms:

```
aws cloudtrail update-trail --name <trail> --cloud-watch-logs-log-group-arn <group-arn> --cloud-watch-logs-role-arn <role-arn>
```

Add alarms for root usage, IAM policy changes, and console sign-in failures. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: No Security Incident Alarm exist for AWS Services
items.description: Ensure that Security Incident Alarms are created in CloudWatch.
items.how_to_find:
List existing metric filters and alarms and compare against the CIS monitoring set (unauthorized API calls, root usage, IAM changes, SG/NACL/route changes, CMK deletion):

```
aws logs describe-metric-filters --log-group-name <trail-group> --query 'metricFilters[].filterName'
aws cloudwatch describe-alarms --query 'MetricAlarms[].AlarmName' --output table
```

Missing CIS alarms are findings. Automated: Prowler `cloudwatch_log_metric_filter_*`.
items.how_to_fix:
Create the CIS metric filters + alarms wired to an SNS topic. Example (root usage):

```
aws logs put-metric-filter --log-group-name <trail-group> --filter-name RootUsage --filter-pattern '{ $.userIdentity.type = "Root" && $.eventType != "AwsServiceEvent" }' --metric-transformations metricName=RootUsage,metricNamespace=CIS,metricValue=1
aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm --alarm-name RootUsage --namespace CIS --metric-name RootUsage --statistic Sum --period 300 --threshold 1 --comparison-operator GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold --evaluation-periods 1 --alarm-actions <sns-arn>
```

Deploy the full CIS set via IaC. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: RDS Database instance are Publicly accessible
items.description: Ensure RDS database instances are not publicly accessible and prone to security risks.
items.how_to_find:
List instances with `PubliclyAccessible=true`:

```
aws rds describe-db-instances --query "DBInstances[?PubliclyAccessible==`true`].[DBInstanceIdentifier,Endpoint.Address]" --output table
```

Combined with an open SG, this exposes the DB to the internet. Automated: Prowler `rds_instance_no_public_access`.
items.how_to_fix:
Disable public accessibility and place the DB in private subnets behind tight SGs:

```
aws rds modify-db-instance --db-instance-identifier <id> --no-publicly-accessible --apply-immediately
```

Restrict the DB security group to app subnets only. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Deletion protection is not Enabled for RDS Instance
items.description: Ensure Deletion Protection feature is enabled for your AWS RDS database instances.
items.how_to_find:
Check `DeletionProtection` per instance:

```
aws rds describe-db-instances --query 'DBInstances[].[DBInstanceIdentifier,DeletionProtection]' --output table
```

`false` on production DBs is a finding.
items.how_to_fix:
Enable deletion protection:

```
aws rds modify-db-instance --db-instance-identifier <id> --deletion-protection --apply-immediately
```

Set `deletion_protection = true` in Terraform for prod databases.

items.title: RDS Automated Backup is not enabled
items.description: Ensure that Automated Backups are created for the RDS Instances.
items.how_to_find:
Check `BackupRetentionPeriod` (0 disables automated backups):

```
aws rds describe-db-instances --query 'DBInstances[].[DBInstanceIdentifier,BackupRetentionPeriod]' --output table
```

`0` = finding. Automated: Prowler `rds_instance_backup_enabled`.
items.how_to_fix:
Enable automated backups with an adequate retention window (>=7, ideally 30 days):

```
aws rds modify-db-instance --db-instance-identifier <id> --backup-retention-period 30 --preferred-backup-window 03:00-04:00 --apply-immediately
```

Consider AWS Backup with cross-region/vault-lock for critical data. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: RDS Instance Encryption is not Enabled
items.description: Ensure AWS RDS instances are encrypted to meet security and compliance requirements.
items.how_to_find:
List instances where `StorageEncrypted=false`:

```
aws rds describe-db-instances --query "DBInstances[?StorageEncrypted==`false`].[DBInstanceIdentifier,Engine]" --output table
```

Automated: Prowler `rds_instance_storage_encrypted`.
items.how_to_fix:
Storage encryption can't be toggled in place - create an encrypted copy: snapshot, copy the snapshot with a CMK, then restore:

```
aws rds create-db-snapshot --db-instance-identifier <id> --db-snapshot-identifier <snap>
aws rds copy-db-snapshot --source-db-snapshot-identifier <snap> --target-db-snapshot-identifier <snap-enc> --kms-key-id <cmk>
aws rds restore-db-instance-from-db-snapshot --db-instance-identifier <id>-enc --db-snapshot-identifier <snap-enc>
```

Cut over and retire the old instance. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: KMS Customer Master Keys is not used for RDS Instance Encryption
items.description: Ensure RDS instances are encrypted with KMS CMKs in order to have full control over data encryption and decryption.
items.how_to_find:
Check the KMS key used for encrypted instances (default `aws/rds` vs CMK):

```
aws rds describe-db-instances --query 'DBInstances[?StorageEncrypted==`true`].[DBInstanceIdentifier,KmsKeyId]' --output table
```

Default AWS-managed key where a CMK is required is a finding.
items.how_to_fix:
Re-encrypt with a customer-managed CMK via encrypted snapshot copy + restore:

```
aws rds copy-db-snapshot --source-db-snapshot-identifier <snap> --target-db-snapshot-identifier <snap-cmk> --kms-key-id <cmk-arn>
```

Grant RDS usage on the CMK and cut over. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: RDS Snapshots Encryption is not Enabled
items.description: Ensure that AWS RDS snapshots are encrypted to meet security and compliance requirements.
items.how_to_find:
List unencrypted DB snapshots:

```
aws rds describe-db-snapshots --query "DBSnapshots[?Encrypted==`false`].[DBSnapshotIdentifier]" --output table
```

Automated: Prowler `rds_snapshots_encrypted`.
items.how_to_fix:
Create an encrypted copy with a CMK and delete the unencrypted one:

```
aws rds copy-db-snapshot --source-db-snapshot-identifier <snap> --target-db-snapshot-identifier <snap-enc> --kms-key-id <cmk>
aws rds delete-db-snapshot --db-snapshot-identifier <snap>
```

Encrypt source instances so future snapshots inherit encryption. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: RDS Snapshots Publicly accessible
items.description: Ensure that your Amazon RDS database snapshots are not accessible to all AWS accounts.
items.how_to_find:
Find snapshots shared with `all`:

```
aws rds describe-db-snapshots --snapshot-type manual --query 'DBSnapshots[].DBSnapshotIdentifier' --output text | tr '\t' '\n' | while read s; do aws rds describe-db-snapshot-attributes --db-snapshot-identifier "$s" --query "DBSnapshotAttributesResult.DBSnapshotAttributes[?AttributeName=='restore'].AttributeValues" --output text | grep -q all && echo "PUBLIC $s"; done
```

Critical: exposes full DB contents. Automated: Prowler `rds_snapshots_public_access`.
items.how_to_fix:
Remove the public share:

```
aws rds modify-db-snapshot-attribute --db-snapshot-identifier <snap> --attribute-name restore --values-to-remove all
```

Share only to specific account IDs and rotate any exposed data/credentials. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: RDS Log Exports is not Enabled (RDS MySQL, Aurora and MariaDB)
items.description: Ensure Log Exports feature is enabled for your AWS RDS MySQL, Aurora and MariaDB database instances.
items.how_to_find:
Check which log types are exported to CloudWatch:

```
aws rds describe-db-instances --query 'DBInstances[].[DBInstanceIdentifier,EnabledCloudwatchLogsExports]' --output table
```

Empty/`null` (no error/audit/slow/general logs) is a finding.
items.how_to_fix:
Enable log exports (engine-specific) to CloudWatch Logs:

```
aws rds modify-db-instance --db-instance-identifier <id> --cloudwatch-logs-export-configuration '{"EnableLogTypes":["audit","error","general","slowquery"]}' --apply-immediately
```

Enable the DB audit plugin/option group where needed and ship to your SIEM.

items.title: IAM Database Authentication is not Enabled for RDS Instances
items.description: Ensure IAM Database Authentication feature is enabled for your AWS RDS MySQL and PostgreSQL database instances.
items.how_to_find:
Check `IAMDatabaseAuthenticationEnabled`:

```
aws rds describe-db-instances --query 'DBInstances[].[DBInstanceIdentifier,IAMDatabaseAuthenticationEnabled]' --output table
```

`false` = finding (relies on static DB passwords). Automated: Prowler `rds_instance_iam_authentication_enabled`.
items.how_to_fix:
Enable IAM DB auth and grant DB users the `rds_iam` role:

```
aws rds modify-db-instance --db-instance-identifier <id> --enable-iam-database-authentication --apply-immediately
```

Apps then fetch short-lived tokens via `aws rds generate-db-auth-token`. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: RDS Auto Minor Version Upgrade Not Enabled
items.description: Ensure AWS RDS instances have the Auto Minor Version Upgrade feature enabled.
items.how_to_find:
Check `AutoMinorVersionUpgrade`:

```
aws rds describe-db-instances --query 'DBInstances[].[DBInstanceIdentifier,AutoMinorVersionUpgrade]' --output table
```

`false` = finding (missing security patches).
items.how_to_fix:
Enable automatic minor version upgrades within a maintenance window:

```
aws rds modify-db-instance --db-instance-identifier <id> --auto-minor-version-upgrade --preferred-maintenance-window sun:04:00-sun:05:00 --apply-immediately
```

Track major-version EOL separately. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: RDS Database Instance not updated
items.description: Ensure that the RDS Instance is Updated. Good hygiene that pays off over time.
items.how_to_find:
List engine versions and compare against the latest supported / non-EOL versions:

```
aws rds describe-db-instances --query 'DBInstances[].[DBInstanceIdentifier,Engine,EngineVersion]' --output table
aws rds describe-db-engine-versions --engine <engine> --query 'DBEngineVersions[-1].EngineVersion'
```

Old/EOL engine versions are findings.
items.how_to_fix:
Plan and apply a version upgrade (test in staging first):

```
aws rds modify-db-instance --db-instance-identifier <id> --engine-version <target> --allow-major-version-upgrade --apply-immediately
```

Snapshot before upgrading and validate the app afterwards.

items.title: RDS Automated Backup is not enabled
items.description: Ensure AWS RDS instances have Automated Backups feature enabled.
items.how_to_find:
Check `BackupRetentionPeriod` (0 disables automated backups):

```
aws rds describe-db-instances --query 'DBInstances[].[DBInstanceIdentifier,BackupRetentionPeriod]' --output table
```

`0` = finding. Automated: Prowler `rds_instance_backup_enabled`.
items.how_to_fix:
Enable automated backups with an adequate retention window (>=7, ideally 30 days):

```
aws rds modify-db-instance --db-instance-identifier <id> --backup-retention-period 30 --preferred-backup-window 03:00-04:00 --apply-immediately
```

Consider AWS Backup with cross-region/vault-lock for critical data. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: RDS Secure Transport is not Enabled (SQL Server, PostgreSQL)
items.description: Ensure AWS RDS SQL Server instances have Transport Encryption feature enabled.
items.how_to_find:
Check the parameter group enforces TLS (`rds.force_ssl=1` for PostgreSQL / `rds.force_ssl` for SQL Server):

```
aws rds describe-db-parameters --db-parameter-group-name <pg> --query "Parameters[?ParameterName=='rds.force_ssl'].[ParameterValue]" --output text
```

Not `1` = finding.
items.how_to_fix:
Set `rds.force_ssl=1` in the parameter group and reboot to apply:

```
aws rds modify-db-parameter-group --db-parameter-group-name <pg> --parameters "ParameterName=rds.force_ssl,ParameterValue=1,ApplyMethod=pending-reboot"
aws rds reboot-db-instance --db-instance-identifier <id>
```

Require the RDS CA in client connection strings. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: RDS Backup Retention Period is not enough
items.description: Ensure AWS RDS instances have sufficient backup retention period for compliance purposes.
items.how_to_find:
Check retention against policy (e.g. >=7 or >=30 days):

```
aws rds describe-db-instances --query 'DBInstances[].[DBInstanceIdentifier,BackupRetentionPeriod]' --output table
```

Values below policy are findings. Automated: Prowler `rds_instance_backup_enabled`.
items.how_to_fix:
Increase retention and add AWS Backup with vault lock for long-term/immutable copies:

```
aws rds modify-db-instance --db-instance-identifier <id> --backup-retention-period 30 --apply-immediately
```

Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: SSL/TLS Certificates Already Expired for RDS
items.description: Ensure that RDS Instance is using the updated SSL Certificate.
items.how_to_find:
Check the CA certificate identifier on each instance; older CAs (e.g. rds-ca-2015) are expired/deprecated:

```
aws rds describe-db-instances --query 'DBInstances[].[DBInstanceIdentifier,CACertificateIdentifier]' --output table
aws rds describe-certificates --query 'Certificates[].[CertificateIdentifier,ValidTill]' --output table
```
items.how_to_fix:
Rotate to the current CA bundle (e.g. `rds-ca-rsa2048-g1`) during a maintenance window and update client trust stores:

```
aws rds modify-db-instance --db-instance-identifier <id> --ca-certificate-identifier rds-ca-rsa2048-g1 --apply-immediately
```

Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: RDS not using Multi-AZ deployment
items.description: Ensure AWS RDS clusters have the Multi-AZ feature enabled.
items.how_to_find:
Check `MultiAZ`:

```
aws rds describe-db-instances --query 'DBInstances[].[DBInstanceIdentifier,MultiAZ]' --output table
```

`false` on production DBs is an availability finding.
items.how_to_fix:
Convert production instances to Multi-AZ:

```
aws rds modify-db-instance --db-instance-identifier <id> --multi-az --apply-immediately
```

Set `multi_az = true` in Terraform for prod. Test failover afterwards.

items.title: Cross-Account Access for SNS Topics
items.description: Ensure Amazon SNS topics do not allow unknown cross account access.
items.how_to_find:
Inspect each topic policy for external principals:

```
for t in $(aws sns list-topics --query 'Topics[].TopicArn' --output text); do echo "=== $t ==="; aws sns get-topic-attributes --topic-arn "$t" --query 'Attributes.Policy' --output text | python3 -m json.tool | grep -A2 Principal; done
```

External account ARNs without a condition are findings.
items.how_to_fix:
Scope the topic policy to known principals and add `aws:PrincipalOrgID`/`aws:SourceArn` conditions. Apply with:

```
aws sns set-topic-attributes --topic-arn <t> --attribute-name Policy --attribute-value file://policy.json
```

Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: SNS Topics Exposed to Everyone
items.description: Ensure that AWS Simple Notification Service (SNS) topics are not exposed to everyone.
items.how_to_find:
Find topic policies with `"Principal":"*"` and no restricting condition:

```
for t in $(aws sns list-topics --query 'Topics[].TopicArn' --output text); do aws sns get-topic-attributes --topic-arn "$t" --query 'Attributes.Policy' --output text | grep -q '"Principal":"\*"' && echo "PUBLIC $t"; done
```

Critical if it allows `Publish`/`Subscribe` to anyone. Automated: Prowler `sns_topics_not_publicly_accessible`.
items.how_to_fix:
Remove the public statement or add a hard condition (`aws:SourceOwner`/`aws:PrincipalOrgID`):

```
aws sns set-topic-attributes --topic-arn <t> --attribute-name Policy --attribute-value file://scoped-policy.json
```

Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Server-Side Encryption is Not Enabled for AWS SNS Topics
items.description: Ensure that Amazon SNS topics enforce Server-Side Encryption (SSE).
items.how_to_find:
Check `KmsMasterKeyId` per topic:

```
for t in $(aws sns list-topics --query 'Topics[].TopicArn' --output text); do echo "$t -> $(aws sns get-topic-attributes --topic-arn "$t" --query 'Attributes.KmsMasterKeyId' --output text)"; done
```

`None` = finding. Automated: Prowler `sns_topics_kms_encryption_at_rest_enabled`.
items.how_to_fix:
Enable SSE with a CMK:

```
aws sns set-topic-attributes --topic-arn <t> --attribute-name KmsMasterKeyId --attribute-value <cmk-id>
```

Grant SNS usage on the key. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: KMS Customer Master Keys is not used for SNS Topics Encryption
items.description: Ensure that Amazon SNS topics are encrypted with KMS Customer Master Keys (CMKs).
items.how_to_find:
Confirm topics use a customer-managed CMK, not the default `alias/aws/sns`:

```
aws sns get-topic-attributes --topic-arn <t> --query 'Attributes.KmsMasterKeyId'
```

Default key where a CMK is required is a finding.
items.how_to_fix:
Set a customer-managed CMK and restrict its key policy:

```
aws sns set-topic-attributes --topic-arn <t> --attribute-name KmsMasterKeyId --attribute-value arn:aws:kms:<region>:<acct>:key/<id>
```

Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Lack of KMS Key Rotation
items.description: Ensure KMS key rotation feature is enabled for all your Customer Master Keys (CMK).
items.how_to_find:
Check automatic annual rotation on customer-managed keys:

```
for k in $(aws kms list-keys --query 'Keys[].KeyId' --output text); do mgr=$(aws kms describe-key --key-id "$k" --query 'KeyMetadata.KeyManager' --output text); [ "$mgr" = "CUSTOMER" ] && echo "$k rotation=$(aws kms get-key-rotation-status --key-id "$k" --query 'KeyRotationEnabled')"; done
```

`false` = finding. Automated: Prowler `kms_cmk_rotation_enabled`.
items.how_to_fix:
Enable automatic rotation:

```
aws kms enable-key-rotation --key-id <cmk>
```

For imported key material, rotate manually on schedule. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: AWS Keys Exposed to Everyone
items.description: Ensure Amazon KMS master keys are not exposed to everyone.
items.how_to_find:
Inspect key policies for `"Principal":"*"`:

```
for k in $(aws kms list-keys --query 'Keys[].KeyId' --output text); do aws kms get-key-policy --key-id "$k" --policy-name default --query Policy --output text | grep -q '"AWS": "\*"' && echo "PUBLIC $k"; done
```

Critical: anyone could use/manage the key. Automated: Prowler `kms_key_not_publicly_accessible`.
items.how_to_fix:
Rewrite the key policy to grant only specific roles and add `aws:PrincipalOrgID`:

```
aws kms put-key-policy --key-id <cmk> --policy-name default --policy file://key-policy.json
```

Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Cross-Account Access for KMS Service
items.description: Ensure Amazon KMS master keys do not allow unknown cross account access.
items.how_to_find:
Review key policies and grants for external accounts:

```
aws kms get-key-policy --key-id <cmk> --policy-name default --query Policy --output text | python3 -m json.tool | grep -A2 Principal
aws kms list-grants --key-id <cmk> --query 'Grants[].[GranteePrincipal,Operations]'
```

External principals without justification are findings.
items.how_to_fix:
Limit cross-account use to specific role ARNs and required operations only; retire unused grants:

```
aws kms retire-grant --key-id <cmk> --grant-id <id>
```

Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Code Signing is not Enabled for Lambda Functions
items.description: Ensure that Code Signing is enabled for your Amazon Lambda functions.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether functions have a code-signing config:

```
for f in $(aws lambda list-functions --query 'Functions[].FunctionName' --output text); do echo "$f -> $(aws lambda get-function-code-signing-config --function-name "$f" --query 'CodeSigningConfigArn' --output text 2>/dev/null)"; done
```

`None` = finding. Automated: Prowler `awslambda_function_using_supported_runtimes` family.
items.how_to_fix:
Create a Signer profile + code-signing config (enforce), then attach:

```
aws lambda create-code-signing-config --allowed-publishers SigningProfileVersionArns=<arn> --code-signing-policies UntrustedArtifactOnDeployment=Enforce
aws lambda put-function-code-signing-config --function-name <f> --code-signing-config-arn <csc-arn>
```

Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Lambda Runtime Environment Version is not Latest
items.description: Ensure that the latest version of the runtime environment is used for your AWS Lambda functions.
items.how_to_find:
List function runtimes and flag deprecated ones (e.g. old `nodejs`, `python3.7`):

```
aws lambda list-functions --query 'Functions[].[FunctionName,Runtime]' --output table
```

Deprecated/EOL runtimes are findings. Automated: Prowler `awslambda_function_using_supported_runtimes`.
items.how_to_fix:
Upgrade code to a supported runtime and redeploy:

```
aws lambda update-function-configuration --function-name <f> --runtime python3.12
```

Track runtime deprecation notices and automate upgrades in CI. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Cross-Account Access for Lambda Functions Queues
items.description: Ensure AWS Lambda functions do not allow unknown cross account access via permission policies.
items.how_to_find:
Inspect resource policies for external-account grants:

```
aws lambda get-policy --function-name <f> --query Policy --output text | python3 -m json.tool
```

Statements with external `Principal` and no `SourceArn`/`SourceAccount` condition are findings.
items.how_to_fix:
Scope each permission to a specific source and account:

```
aws lambda add-permission --function-name <f> --statement-id <sid> --action lambda:InvokeFunction --principal <svc-or-acct> --source-arn <arn> --source-account <acct>
```

Remove overly broad statements with `remove-permission`. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Lambda Function Exposed to Everyone
items.description: Ensure that your Amazon Lambda functions are not exposed to everyone.
items.how_to_find:
Find functions whose resource policy allows `Principal:"*"`:

```
aws lambda get-policy --function-name <f> --query Policy --output text | grep -q '"AWS":"\*"' && echo "PUBLIC <f>"
```

Critical if anyone can invoke it. Automated: Prowler `awslambda_function_not_publicly_accessible`.
items.how_to_fix:
Remove the public statement and re-add a scoped one:

```
aws lambda remove-permission --function-name <f> --statement-id <public-sid>
```

Front the function with API Gateway + authorizer if public reach is required. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Lambda Environment Variables are not Encrypted
items.description: Ensure encryption is enabled for the AWS Lambda environment variables that store sensitive information.
items.how_to_find:
Check for a customer-managed `KMSKeyArn` on function config and scan env vars for plaintext secrets:

```
aws lambda get-function-configuration --function-name <f> --query '[KMSKeyArn,Environment.Variables]'
```

No CMK + secrets in env = finding.
items.how_to_fix:
Move secrets to Secrets Manager / SSM Parameter Store (SecureString) and reference them at runtime; encrypt remaining env vars with a CMK:

```
aws lambda update-function-configuration --function-name <f> --kms-key-arn <cmk-arn>
```

Rotate any exposed secrets. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: KMS Customer Master Keys is not used for Lambda Environment Variables Encryption
items.description: Ensure Lambda environment variables are encrypted with KMS Customer Master Keys (CMKs) to gain full control over data encryption and decryption.
items.how_to_find:
Confirm a customer-managed CMK encrypts env vars (default AWS-managed key otherwise):

```
aws lambda get-function-configuration --function-name <f> --query 'KMSKeyArn'
```

`null` = finding where a CMK is required.
items.how_to_fix:
Attach a CMK and grant Lambda usage in the key policy:

```
aws lambda update-function-configuration --function-name <f> --kms-key-arn arn:aws:kms:<region>:<acct>:key/<id>
```

Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: AWS Config Not Used
items.description: Ensure AWS Config is enabled in all regions to get the optimal visibility of the activity on your account.
items.how_to_find:
Check for an active configuration recorder in each region:

```
aws configservice describe-configuration-recorder-status --query 'ConfigurationRecordersStatus[].[name,recording]' --output table
```

Empty/`recording=false` = finding. Automated: Prowler `config_recorder_all_regions_enabled`.
items.how_to_fix:
Enable Config org-wide (all resource types + global) with a delivery channel to S3:

```
aws configservice put-configuration-recorder --configuration-recorder name=default,roleARN=<role>,recordingGroup='{"allSupported":true,"includeGlobalResourceTypes":true}'
aws configservice start-configuration-recorder --configuration-recorder-name default
```

Deploy via CloudFormation StackSets to all regions/accounts. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Validate AWS Config Rules
items.description: Validate the AWS Config Rules and check for NonCompliant Rules.
items.how_to_find:
List deployed Config rules and their compliance:

```
aws configservice describe-config-rules --query 'ConfigRules[].ConfigRuleName' --output table
aws configservice describe-compliance-by-config-rule --query "ComplianceByConfigRules[?Compliance.ComplianceType=='NON_COMPLIANT'].[ConfigRuleName]" --output table
```

Missing conformance packs (CIS/Foundational) or many NON_COMPLIANT rules are findings.
items.how_to_fix:
Deploy managed conformance packs and remediate non-compliant rules (some support auto-remediation via SSM):

```
aws configservice put-conformance-pack --conformance-pack-name CIS --template-s3-uri s3://<bucket>/cis-pack.yaml
```

Wire auto-remediation for high-value rules. Re-scan.

items.title: AWS GuardDuty Not Used
items.description: Ensure Amazon GuardDuty is enabled to help you protect your AWS accounts and workloads against security threats.
items.how_to_find:
Check for an enabled detector per region:

```
aws guardduty list-detectors --query 'DetectorIds' --output text
```

Empty = finding (no threat detection). Automated: Prowler `guardduty_is_enabled`.
items.how_to_fix:
Enable GuardDuty (delegated admin, org auto-enable) in all regions:

```
aws guardduty create-detector --enable --finding-publishing-frequency FIFTEEN_MINUTES
```

Turn on S3, EKS, Malware, and RDS protection plans; route findings to Security Hub + SNS. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Validate the AWS GuardDuty Findings
items.description: Always validate the findings that are reported by GuardDuty.
items.how_to_find:
List current findings by severity:

```
DET=$(aws guardduty list-detectors --query 'DetectorIds[0]' --output text)
aws guardduty list-findings --detector-id "$DET" --finding-criteria '{"Criterion":{"severity":{"Gte":7}}}'
```

Unreviewed high/critical findings are the issue.
items.how_to_fix:
Triage high/critical findings, archive false positives, and wire auto-response:

```
aws guardduty get-findings --detector-id "$DET" --finding-ids <id>
```

Integrate with Security Hub + EventBridge to auto-isolate compromised instances/keys. Establish an on-call runbook.

items.title: Route 53 Domain Transfer Lock is not Enabled
items.description: Ensure your domain names have the Transfer Lock feature enabled in order to keep them secure.
items.how_to_find:
Check the transfer lock on each registered domain:

```
for d in $(aws route53domains list-domains --region us-east-1 --query 'Domains[].DomainName' --output text); do echo "$d lock=$(aws route53domains get-domain-detail --region us-east-1 --domain-name "$d" --query 'StatusList' --output text)"; done
```

Missing `clientTransferProhibited` is a finding (domain hijack risk).
items.how_to_fix:
Enable the registrar transfer lock:

```
aws route53domains enable-domain-transfer-lock --region us-east-1 --domain-name <domain>
```

Also enable registrant-change and auto-renew protections.

items.title: SPF Record not Present
items.description: Ensure there is an SPF record set for each MX DNS record in order to stop spammers from spoofing your domains.
items.how_to_find:
Check for an SPF TXT record on sending domains:

```
dig +short TXT example.com | grep -i 'v=spf1'
```

No `v=spf1` record is a finding (spoofing risk).
items.how_to_fix:
Publish SPF, and pair with DKIM + DMARC. Example records:

```
example.com. TXT "v=spf1 include:amazonses.com -all"
_dmarc.example.com. TXT "v=DMARC1; p=reject; rua=mailto:dmarc@example.com"
```

Create via `aws route53 change-resource-record-sets`. Verify with `dig TXT`.

items.title: Route53 Domains Already Expired
items.description: Ensure expired AWS Route 53 domains names are restored.
items.how_to_find:
List domains whose expiry date is in the past:

```
aws route53domains list-domains --region us-east-1 --query 'Domains[].[DomainName,Expiry]' --output table
```

Any past-due `Expiry` is critical (loss of domain).
items.how_to_fix:
Renew immediately (if within the grace/redemption window) and enable auto-renew:

```
aws route53domains renew-domain --region us-east-1 --domain-name <domain> --duration-in-years 1 --current-expiry-year <year>
aws route53domains enable-domain-auto-renew --region us-east-1 --domain-name <domain>
```

items.title: Route53 Domains are about to Expire
items.description: Ensure AWS Route 53 domain names are renewed before their expiration (90 days before expiration).
items.how_to_find:
Flag domains expiring within ~45 days:

```
aws route53domains list-domains --region us-east-1 --query 'Domains[].[DomainName,Expiry,AutoRenew]' --output table
```

Near-term `Expiry` with `AutoRenew=false` is a finding.
items.how_to_fix:
Enable auto-renew and confirm valid billing/contacts:

```
aws route53domains enable-domain-auto-renew --region us-east-1 --domain-name <domain>
```

Add an EventBridge/CloudWatch reminder ahead of expiry.

items.title: DNSSEC Signing for Route 53 Hosted Zones is not Enabled
items.description: Ensure that DNSSEC signing is enabled for your Amazon Route 53 Hosted Zones.
items.how_to_find:
Check DNSSEC status per hosted zone:

```
aws route53 get-dnssec --hosted-zone-id <zone-id> --query 'Status'
```

`NOT_SIGNING` = finding (DNS spoofing/cache-poisoning risk).
items.how_to_fix:
Enable DNSSEC signing and add the DS record at the registrar:

```
aws route53 create-key-signing-key --hosted-zone-id <zone> --key-management-service-arn <cmk> --name ksk1 --status ACTIVE
aws route53 enable-hosted-zone-dnssec --hosted-zone-id <zone>
```

Publish the DS record to the parent zone/registrar to complete the chain of trust.

items.title: EKS Secret Encryption is not Enabled
items.description: Ensure that envelope encryption of Kubernetes secrets using Amazon KMS is enabled.
items.how_to_find:
Check for KMS envelope encryption of Kubernetes secrets:

```
aws eks describe-cluster --name <cluster> --query 'cluster.encryptionConfig'
```

`null` = finding (etcd secrets not encrypted with a CMK). Automated: Prowler `eks_cluster_kms_cmk_encryption_in_secrets_enabled`.
items.how_to_fix:
Enable secrets encryption with a CMK (can be added to existing clusters):

```
aws eks associate-encryption-config --cluster-name <cluster> --encryption-config '[{"resources":["secrets"],"provider":{"keyArn":"<cmk-arn>"}}]'
```

Grant EKS usage on the key. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Kubernetes Cluster Logging is not Enabled
items.description: Ensure that EKS control plane logging is enabled for your Amazon EKS clusters.
items.how_to_find:
Check enabled control-plane log types:

```
aws eks describe-cluster --name <cluster> --query 'cluster.logging.clusterLogging'
```

`api`/`audit`/`authenticator` disabled is a finding. Automated: Prowler `eks_control_plane_logging_all_types_enabled`.
items.how_to_fix:
Enable all control-plane log types to CloudWatch:

```
aws eks update-cluster-config --name <cluster> --logging '{"clusterLogging":[{"types":["api","audit","authenticator","controllerManager","scheduler"],"enabled":true}]}'
```

Ship audit logs to your SIEM. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Kubernetes Cluster Version is not Updated
items.description: Ensure that the latest version of Kubernetes is installed on your Amazon EKS clusters.
items.how_to_find:
Compare the cluster version to the latest EKS-supported version:

```
aws eks describe-cluster --name <cluster> --query 'cluster.version'
aws eks describe-addon-versions --query 'addons[0].addonVersions[0]' 2>/dev/null
```

Versions near/past end-of-support are findings.
items.how_to_fix:
Upgrade the control plane, then node groups and add-ons, one minor version at a time (test first):

```
aws eks update-cluster-version --name <cluster> --kubernetes-version <target>
aws eks update-nodegroup-version --cluster-name <cluster> --nodegroup-name <ng>
```

Validate workloads after each step.

items.title: Cluster Endpoints are Publicly accessible
items.description: Ensure that AWS EKS cluster endpoint access is not public and prone to security risks.
items.how_to_find:
Check the API server endpoint access config:

```
aws eks describe-cluster --name <cluster> --query 'cluster.resourcesVpcConfig.[endpointPublicAccess,endpointPrivateAccess,publicAccessCidrs]'
```

`endpointPublicAccess=true` with `publicAccessCidrs=0.0.0.0/0` is critical. Automated: Prowler `eks_endpoints_not_publicly_accessible`.
items.how_to_fix:
Make the endpoint private (or restrict public CIDRs) and enable private access:

```
aws eks update-cluster-config --name <cluster> --resources-vpc-config endpointPublicAccess=false,endpointPrivateAccess=true
```

If public access is required, restrict `publicAccessCidrs` to admin IPs. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Server-Side Encryption is not Enabled for SQS Queues
items.description: Ensure Amazon SQS queues enforce Server-Side Encryption (SSE).
items.how_to_find:
Check SSE attributes per queue:

```
for q in $(aws sqs list-queues --query 'QueueUrls' --output text); do echo "$q -> $(aws sqs get-queue-attributes --queue-url "$q" --attribute-names KmsMasterKeyId SqsManagedSseEnabled --query 'Attributes')"; done
```

Neither `KmsMasterKeyId` nor `SqsManagedSseEnabled=true` = finding. Automated: Prowler `sqs_queues_server_side_encryption_enabled`.
items.how_to_fix:
Enable SSE (SSE-KMS with a CMK preferred):

```
aws sqs set-queue-attributes --queue-url <q> --attributes KmsMasterKeyId=<cmk>
```

Or `SqsManagedSseEnabled=true` for SSE-SQS. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: KMS Customer Master Keys is not used for SQS Queue Encryption
items.description: Ensure SQS queues are encrypted with KMS CMKs to gain full control over data encryption and decryption.
items.how_to_find:
Confirm the queue uses a customer-managed CMK, not SSE-SQS/default:

```
aws sqs get-queue-attributes --queue-url <q> --attribute-names KmsMasterKeyId --query 'Attributes.KmsMasterKeyId'
```

Missing/default = finding where a CMK is required.
items.how_to_fix:
Set a customer-managed CMK and grant SQS usage on it:

```
aws sqs set-queue-attributes --queue-url <q> --attributes KmsMasterKeyId=arn:aws:kms:<region>:<acct>:key/<id>
```

Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: SQS Queue Exposed to Everyone
items.description: Ensure that AWS Simple Queue Service (SQS) queues are not exposed to everyone.
items.how_to_find:
Find queue policies allowing `Principal:"*"`:

```
for q in $(aws sqs list-queues --query 'QueueUrls' --output text); do aws sqs get-queue-attributes --queue-url "$q" --attribute-names Policy --query 'Attributes.Policy' --output text | grep -q '"Principal":"\*"' && echo "PUBLIC $q"; done
```

Critical if anyone can send/receive. Automated: Prowler `sqs_queues_not_publicly_accessible`.
items.how_to_fix:
Replace with a scoped policy (specific principals + `aws:SourceArn`/`aws:PrincipalOrgID`):

```
aws sqs set-queue-attributes --queue-url <q> --attributes Policy=file://scoped-policy.json
```

Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Cross-Account Access for SQS Queues
items.description: Ensure AWS Simple Queue Service (SQS) queues do not allow unknown cross account access.
items.how_to_find:
Review queue policies for external-account principals:

```
aws sqs get-queue-attributes --queue-url <q> --attribute-names Policy --query 'Attributes.Policy' --output text | python3 -m json.tool
```

External ARNs without conditions are findings.
items.how_to_fix: Limit to known accounts and add an `aws:SourceArn` condition tying to the producing resource. Apply via `set-queue-attributes`. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Continuous Backup is Not Enabled for DynamoDB
items.description: Ensure that continous backup is enabld for all the DynamoDB.
items.how_to_find:
Check point-in-time recovery (PITR) per table:

```
for t in $(aws dynamodb list-tables --query 'TableNames' --output text); do echo "$t -> $(aws dynamodb describe-continuous-backups --table-name "$t" --query 'ContinuousBackupsDescription.PointInTimeRecoveryDescription.PointInTimeRecoveryStatus' --output text)"; done
```

`DISABLED` = finding. Automated: Prowler `dynamodb_tables_pitr_enabled`.
items.how_to_fix:
Enable PITR (35-day continuous backup):

```
aws dynamodb update-continuous-backups --table-name <t> --point-in-time-recovery-specification PointInTimeRecoveryEnabled=true
```

Add AWS Backup plans for longer retention. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: KMS Customer Master Keys is not used for DynamoDB Table Encryption
items.description: Ensure that for all the DynamoDB tables are using KMS Customers Master Keys for encryption.
items.how_to_find:
Check the SSE type per table (default `AES256`/AWS-owned vs CMK):

```
aws dynamodb describe-table --table-name <t> --query 'Table.SSEDescription'
```

Missing `KMSMasterKeyArn` (AWS-owned key) where a CMK is required is a finding.
items.how_to_fix:
Switch to a customer-managed CMK:

```
aws dynamodb update-table --table-name <t> --sse-specification Enabled=true,SSEType=KMS,KMSMasterKeyId=<cmk>
```

Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: AWS Backup Vault is Not Prevented from Accidental Deletion
items.description: Ensure that Accidental Deletion is enabled for AWS Backup Vault.
items.how_to_find:
Check for a vault access policy / Vault Lock preventing deletion:

```
aws backup get-backup-vault-access-policy --backup-vault-name <vault> 2>/dev/null
aws backup describe-backup-vault --backup-vault-name <vault> --query '[Locked,MinRetentionDays]'
```

No deny-delete policy and `Locked=false` are findings.
items.how_to_fix:
Apply a vault access policy denying `DeleteRecoveryPoint`/`DeleteBackupVault`, then enable Vault Lock (compliance mode) for immutability:

```
aws backup put-backup-vault-access-policy --backup-vault-name <vault> --policy file://deny-delete.json
aws backup put-backup-vault-lock-configuration --backup-vault-name <vault> --min-retention-days 30 --changeable-for-days 3
```

Verify `Locked=true` after the cooling-off period.

items.title: Redshift Cluster is publicly accessible
items.description: Ensure that Redshift Cluster is not publicly accessible.
items.how_to_find:
Check `PubliclyAccessible` on each cluster:

```
aws redshift describe-clusters --query "Clusters[?PubliclyAccessible==`true`].[ClusterIdentifier,Endpoint.Address]" --output table
```

Public + open SG is critical. Automated: Prowler `redshift_cluster_public_access`.
items.how_to_fix:
Disable public accessibility and place the cluster in private subnets:

```
aws redshift modify-cluster --cluster-identifier <id> --no-publicly-accessible
```

Restrict the cluster SG to app CIDRs and enable enhanced VPC routing. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Workspaces Volume Encryption is not Enabled
items.description: Ensure that the volume encryption is enabled for all the Workspaces.
items.how_to_find:
Check root/user volume encryption per WorkSpace:

```
aws workspaces describe-workspaces --query 'Workspaces[].[WorkspaceId,UserVolumeEncryptionEnabled,RootVolumeEncryptionEnabled]' --output table
```

`false` = finding.
items.how_to_fix:
Encryption must be set at creation - recreate the WorkSpace with encryption enabled (back up user data first):

```
aws workspaces create-workspaces --workspaces '[{"DirectoryId":"<d>","UserName":"<u>","BundleId":"<b>","UserVolumeEncryptionEnabled":true,"RootVolumeEncryptionEnabled":true,"VolumeEncryptionKey":"<cmk>"}]'
```

Enforce encryption in your provisioning automation.

items.title: Older Version of ElastiCache Engine in Use
items.description: Ensure that you are not using an older version of elasticache and use the latest version that is available.
items.how_to_find:
List cache clusters and engine versions:

```
aws elasticache describe-cache-clusters --query 'CacheClusters[].[CacheClusterId,Engine,EngineVersion]' --output table
```

Old/EOL Redis/Memcached versions are findings.
items.how_to_fix:
Upgrade to a current engine version in a maintenance window (test first):

```
aws elasticache modify-cache-cluster --cache-cluster-id <id> --engine-version <target> --apply-immediately
```

For Redis, prefer the latest 7.x with TLS + AUTH. Re-scan.

items.title: ElastiCache Redis cluster In-Transit and At-rest Encryption not enabled
items.description: Ensure that redis clusters data In-transit and At-rest encryptions are enabled.
items.how_to_find:
Check both encryption flags on replication groups:

```
aws elasticache describe-replication-groups --query 'ReplicationGroups[].[ReplicationGroupId,AtRestEncryptionEnabled,TransitEncryptionEnabled]' --output table
```

Either `false` is a finding. Automated: Prowler `elasticache_redis_cluster_*_encryption_enabled`.
items.how_to_fix:
Encryption flags are set at creation - recreate the replication group with both enabled and AUTH/RBAC:

```
aws elasticache create-replication-group --replication-group-id <id> --at-rest-encryption-enabled --transit-encryption-enabled --auth-token <strong-token> ...
```

Migrate data, then cut over clients to the TLS endpoint. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: Access logging disabled for CloudFront
items.description: Ensure that access logging is enabled for Cloudfront.
items.how_to_find:
Check standard/real-time logging on each distribution:

```
for d in $(aws cloudfront list-distributions --query 'DistributionList.Items[].Id' --output text); do echo "$d -> $(aws cloudfront get-distribution-config --id "$d" --query 'DistributionConfig.Logging.Enabled' --output text)"; done
```

`false` = finding. Automated: Prowler `cloudfront_distributions_logging_enabled`.
items.how_to_fix:
Enable logging to a dedicated S3 bucket (or Kinesis for real-time) via the distribution config:

```
aws cloudfront update-distribution --id <d> --distribution-config file://config.json --if-match <etag>
```

Set the `Logging` block `Enabled=true` with a `Bucket`+`Prefix`. Ship to your SIEM. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: WAF is Not Enabled in CloudFront
items.description: Ensure that WAF is enabled for all the available Cloudfront.
items.how_to_find:
Check for an associated WebACL on each distribution:

```
aws cloudfront get-distribution-config --id <d> --query 'DistributionConfig.WebACLId'
```

Empty = finding. Automated: Prowler `cloudfront_distributions_using_waf`.
items.how_to_fix:
Associate a WAFv2 (scope `CLOUDFRONT`, us-east-1) web ACL with managed rules + rate limiting:

```
aws cloudfront update-distribution --id <d> --distribution-config file://config.json --if-match <etag>
```

Set `WebACLId` to the WAFv2 ACL ARN. Re-scan with Prowler.

items.title: TLSv1.0 Supported by CloudFront Distribution
items.description: Ensure that you are using latest TLS version for all Cloudfront.
items.how_to_find:
Check the minimum protocol version in the viewer certificate config:

```
aws cloudfront get-distribution-config --id <d> --query 'DistributionConfig.ViewerCertificate.MinimumProtocolVersion'
```

`TLSv1`/`TLSv1_2016` are findings. Automated: Prowler `cloudfront_distributions_using_deprecated_ssl_protocols`.
items.how_to_fix:
Set a modern minimum protocol version (TLS 1.2_2021+) and use SNI:

```
aws cloudfront update-distribution --id <d> --distribution-config file://config.json --if-match <etag>
```

Set `ViewerCertificate.MinimumProtocolVersion=TLSv1.2_2021`. Re-scan with Prowler.
